description: Apollo 17 Flight Journal - Day 3, part 2: Entering the LM

tokens: America, before you open the equalization valve, we'd like one final reading on the LM/CM Delta-P.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: The equalization valve is built into the CM's forward hatch and permits air to pass in either direction in order to have the pressure on both sides of the hatch be equal.
Prior to this, Mission Control wish to evacuate the tunnel and LM to rid it of any leftover gases.
To the side of the hatch is the Tunnel Vent Valve.
This multiposition valve allows a pressure gauge to monitor the pressure difference across the hatch (the LM/CM Delta-P), or it can vent the tunnel, and hence the LM, to space.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: With this cabin pressure now, it's up to 3.5, almost 3.6.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: The tunnel and LM are now at a pressure that is 3.5 psi lower than the cabin.
Since the cabin's normal pressure is about 5 psi, the tunnel is at only 1.5 psi.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're ready to terminate the charge on battery A.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, Cryo Pressure Indicator to Surge/3 and verify cryo.
See if I got the right one here.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Gordo, I cycled the Cryo Pressure Indicator from up to down, back to Surge/3.
And we got a Master Alarm and there was no O2High Flow with it at all.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, I just did it again to verify it.
And that picked up - it picked up the Master Alarm, although it might be associated with the fact that the surge tank is down and coming back up.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Although, the surge tank shouldn't have caused it.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, let me give you one more try on it.
Well, that time it didn't wait.
All I did was go up to 1 slash 2 and the Alarm came on.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, I went back to surge/3; it did not.
So maybe it's coming on when I go up to 1 slash 2.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I can go up to Pressure Cryo Quantity 1 slash 2 and the Alarm comes on.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gordo, you might think about the fact that we were getting them when the cabin pressure was high after launch.
And there might be some association there, also.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We got to unbolt it down there somewhere.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Repress Package valve, Off; should be Off.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay; Tunnel Vent Valve, LM/Command Module Delta-P.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, we're going to open the Pressure Equalization Valve.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And, Gordy, battery A charge has been stopped and the battery compartment pressure is still reading 0.6.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: [Pause.] Yeah, we'll monitor for 3 minutes now.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: If the Delta-P is 2 psi and the cabin pressure is 5 psi, then the absolute tunnel pressure is 3 psi.
With the Equalization Valve closed, monitoring over a 3-minute period should show little change in that reading which will verify that there are no unexpected leaks.
On the assumption that the CM pressure is stable, then were the Delta-P to increase, air would be leaking out of the tunnel or LM.
Were it to decrease, air would be entering the tunnel/LM, probably from the CM.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We just wanted to verify that no - none of the lights in the matrix were - flashed when you operated that switch and got the Master Alarm.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: That's affirmative, Gordo; none of the lights flashed at all.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: When we get the cabin pressure down, Gordy, here, we might try one more time.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, still holding at 2.0 on the Delta-P.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, we'll open the pressure equalization valve and when the cabin pressure gets to 4.0, hit the Repress O2, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Air from the CM cabin will pass through the valve, through the tunnel and through an open valve in the LM's overhead hatch.
This will raise the pressure in both the tunnel and the LM cabin.
As it does so, the pressure in the CM cabin must drop.
If it goes below 4.0 psi, addition oxygen from the repress package will bring it back up to 5.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Delta-P is about 0.6, we might make it this time.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Delta-P's - Okay, I'm going to open her right up.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: There is now little pressure difference between the CM cabin and the tunnel.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, Houston, the hatch is open.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, the Extend Latch is Engaged.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Preload Selector lever - rotate parallel to the orange stripe.
Preload Handle, torque clockwise to unload the old support beams.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: The probe's loose in the tunnel.
Okay, rotate away from the orange stripe.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, I'm trying putting those things back on now, just for the heck of it.
[Pause.] It's brand new; nice and tight.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, probe umbilicals disconnected and stow.
Yeah, yeah, those are yellow ones.
Preload Handle, position against - against the umbilical connector.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Selector Lever is in the mid position.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, the Release Handle is unlocked.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Ratchet Handle unstowed to the full extension; boost to the first detent.
[Pause.] That's good and tight going back to the first detent.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: [Pause.] That's just like in the simulator.
It comes - pushes me out of the way, as a matter of fact.
I'll get it a little better.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Ratchet Handle pulled to full extension and then ratchet one stroke.
[Pause.] Gets it so it gets it off the thing.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, that's one stroke backwards now.
[Pause.] Okay, Ratchet Handle and Installation Strut are restowed.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Capture Latch Release Handle is rotated 180 degrees and it's back in the recess.
Okay, let's see if it comes out.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And I couldn't see it awhile ago, Let me look - the Probe Strut's in the way.
That's why you can't see it until now.
Where do we want to go with this thing down over here at the...
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Houston, it's a nice clean shear pin release there on top of the probe.
It's nice and clean down there.
There's play around the little button on the end of the probe there too.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: In about 1 minute, we're going to have a site handover.
You'll be talking through Hawaii after that takes place.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Yeah, I think I better ver - verify that, just to make sure.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Hold a minute, Gene - wait a minute.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: At this point, the available recording from the Public Affairs Office no longer carries speech and instead is just radio noise.
The PAO announcer soon mentions that communications have been split into two links.

The original typed technical transcript includes a note that "the CMP aboard America was switched to the GOSS 2 net, for which time-annotated recordings are not available." However, at046:46:58, the PAO announcer will inform the press that the missing audio has been located, and the following is what is played back.
It is of a much inferior quality when compared to the other available recordings.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: [Garble] even getting - Can't see if there's any scratching on here now.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Was that a - We'd like to confirm the tunnel index angle - that's a positive - a plus 1.2, is that right?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I want to check it myself.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Well, Gordo, that's what I read, but I figure it's his privilege.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I was just checking for any scratches on the drogue, but it doesn't look like there is any on there.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: The roll docking index is on a 1.2 - a plus - one point - a plus 1.2.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And - I took a look up there in the docking latch number 4.
Bungee is parallel, but it's not fully extended.
You look down in the end of it you know, and it's not fully extended.
And the - capture - the docking latch itself or the docking lever is loose on the docking ring.
So, it looks like to me that's when I ought to recock and fire it again.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Ron, can you estimate in inches how far down the bungee piston is?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Yeah, it's down about a half an inch.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And, when I take the - and move the handle back a ways and I can take the hook [pause] no I did [laughter].
I took the hook and I pulled it back off the docking ring and then it caught again, so now it won't go back over the docking ring.
Maybe I just lifted the hook off the docking [garble] ring with my finger.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Say, Ron, I would like to caution you again, sticking your finger around or under that hook - there may still be some spring energy stored up there that could release.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: No, I was just touching the top of the hook when I pulled it back off the docking ring.
[Pause.] I pulled it back onto the docking ring, and it looks like it's back in the cocked position now.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: I think we've got the picture.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Do you want me to open the hatch here, Jack, or do you want to - while they're thinking about that I'll get out of the way and come on and open the hatch.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: [Laughter.] Can't get up and can't get down.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: We're having a long conversation here about that latch, Ron.
Why don't you all just press on down the checklist and leave it as it is while we think about it?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: That's what we're doing here Gordo.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Gordo, do you want the O2Heaters 1 and 2, Off, and 3, Auto?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: He's going to open the hatch and IVT.
Gordo, let me give you a little more on that docking latch.
When I looked at it, of course, my handle was flush against the edge and it was also - essentially - locked in position.
But since the bungee was down on the thing I took hold of it, and I felt that the hook itself was loose.
Okay, so I took the handle and the handle will come back - would come back once you pressed the little button, you know, to release the handle.
The handle would come back to about a 45-degree position just like it normally does when you try to cock the latches.

Okay, with the handle back in that 45-degree position, then I grabbed hold of the hook and brought the hook off of the docking ring with my fingers and back to about that 45-degree position again just like it was comparable to the first cocked position.
And that's the way it is right now.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Gordo, I'm running up through the tunnel from America to Challenger.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: One piece of tape coming up shortly, provided I can find the tape.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: I'll tell you, Gordo, it's remarkably clean up here.
It doesn't look like the snowstorm that I remember coming into last time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We tried to leave it all tidied up...
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Hey, you did a good job, friend.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: [Long pause.] Oh, you - straight out there?
Let me see; I want to see.
I want to take a look at it, too.
Hey, there it is; sticking straight out.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Houston, America has a VHF antenna - looks deployed perfectly.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Hey, you guys are upside down in there [laughter].
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: The commander's window has a slight amount of condensation on the - along the lower left edge - that's the left-hand edge, really.
It seems to be just there when the Sun warms the window.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Sounds like the cabin repress is working up there.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Man oh man, did the heart beat go up on that?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: This seems to be a repeat of a stunt that Fred Haise became well known for pulling, thanks to its depiction in the movieApollo 13.
Operation of the LM's Cabin Repress Valve produces a loud bang which is apparently harmless but which startles the other members of the crew.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Turns out - there's Sun out in the AOT.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The Alignment Optical Telescope is a low-power optical instrument intended to allow star sightings to give the guidance system an absolute orientation reference.
Its eyepiece is mounted at head height between where the two LM crew will stand.
Its objective pokes out of the top of the LM cabin, angled 45° up from the horizontal.
Its viewpoint can be rotated into one of six detent positions to allow an all-round swathe of the sky to be viewable when aligning the guidance platform.
Evidently, the objective happened to be pointed roughly towards the Sun when Jack entered the LM.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I'm looking out the AOT, and I see a VHF antenna and part of a rendezvous radar antenna in position 1.
[Long pause.] Position 2 looks right into the radar antenna, and, as I said yesterday, it looks beautiful.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Position 3, I can see the other side of America and very, very clean AOT, very clean.
[Long pause.] Okay, I got a good view of the side of the Service Module, [pause] and you can see these little blisters in the side of the covering there quite distinctly.
I think people were talking about those before.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's the side of - that's the side of the Command Module - looking at that has the little blisters on it.
Got to get oriented up here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Got a great view of the hatch through position 6.
Look up, rather than into the Sun [garble].
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And the CDR's OPS is 5900, that's five-nine-zero-zero.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: During their time on the lunar surface, both LM crewmen will carry an emergency supply of oxygen which will be mounted on top of their backpacks.
This is the Oxygen Purge System (OPS) which consists of two spherical tanks each filled with oxygen to an extremely high pressure.
Their function is to flood a suit with oxygen in case of a major leak, thereby gaining a crewman extra time to return to the safety of the LM.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan and Evans [means Schmitt] at this time have gone into the Lunar Module, and they're going through the housekeeping transfer of items from the Command Module into the Lunar Module and will proceed with the checklist of activating the spacecraft, or Lunar Module, spacecraft communications system, and the communications have been split into two links on Earth, Air-to-Ground 1 for the Command Module, and Air-to-Ground 2 for the Lunar Module.
They're on hot mike or a voice-actuated circuit, so we can hear them run through the items as they accomplish them aboard the Lunar Module Challenger.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: You won't scare me any more if I come on up here with you, will you?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: I got to go back in there.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: 040:24:00 -This is Apollo Control, a correction on the earlier statement, it is Cernan and Schmitt in the Lunar Module Challenger at this time.
Evans had the detail of removing the probe and drogue earlier, but he's by his lonesome, back in the Command Module.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Do you want to stack part of it over there?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: There's strips of the stuff, you know, I'll just give you a strip of it.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: That's nine of them, isn't it?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: [Garble] both circuit breaker panels were as advertised, Gordy.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Am I not looking in the right place?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: I don't know where one is, right offhand.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Well, just help me with some terms here.
I was just looking again at side of - underneath the power bungee.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Gordy; we're at the top of 1-13 and all switches, valves were in proper configuration.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, you could have asked me and I could have told you that.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We were just trying to give you a plug - Well, if you don't want them - Well, we'll take it back.
[Laughter.] [Long pause.] I shouldn't have said anything to the world's most experienced DLMP.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Hey, can you guys open the hatch out there.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Ron, at first glance, it doesn't look like you have one onboard, a picture of the latch.
We're still looking though, but I don't think we have one.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Well, it was kind of the conclusion I came to there, when I didn't find it in the systems data - Hey, I got a little bit to tell you about that.
Oh, those little ones underneath the power bungee.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, if you look at the docking latch number 4 up beside of that - Oh, kind of a J-hook looking thing.
In other words, a great big fat thing with a head on top of it.
And if you consider the fat thing with the head on it as a snowman, well then, the snowman's head is pointing out a 9 o'clock, on that one.
Whereas one that's fully cocked and latched over there, the snowman's head points up at about 11 o'clock.

And [pause], okay, there's a lever that comes right out of the bottom of the power bungee, it looks like, and then it comes out of the bottom, then [garble] of that lever is another silver slot or silver bar that goes from the lever to the J-hook with the snowman on it.
That particular thing that connects the J-hook to the lever coming out of the bottom of the bungee is visible.
In other words, it's not sticking back underneath the J-hook.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Ron, we've got just a line drawing here and we've still haven't got with you on what's what according to the drawing.
Maybe you can hold off until we get a model of the latch, then we can stay with your description.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Yeah, I know it, it's up to you.
[Garble] [Pause] [Garble] I want to float up in the tunnel.
Do you want to float up and look?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Yeah, the congestion I had all day yesterday is just about gone.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Gordy, you'll be happy to know that putting the LMP's camera together is 500-percent easier in zero gravity.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Hey, Jack, if you get a chance, take a picture back this way.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Based on measurements of the image size, two more photographs of Earth are taken around this time showing the Americas.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Putting the cue cards up now, and the camera, by the way, Gordy, operated for two frames and the Reseau looked clean, lens looks clean and everything's fine with it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack is discussing the camera that will be used for taking photos on the lunar surface, not the one he has been using to take pictures of Earth.
The cameras they will use on the Moon are modified versions of the Hasselblad 500C fitted with Reseau plates, which is a glass plate mounted directly in front of the film.
It is engraved with fine crosses 1 centimetre apart that leave an imprint on the image to help with photogrammetry.
The camera he has used for Earth photography is a more basic version of the Hasselblad and does not have a Reseau plate.
All of them, however, have electric winders, then a novelty in still cameras that use film.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Schmitt and Cernan at this time still in the Lunar Module going through the activation and housekeeping chores that are scheduled in the Flight Plan.
They entered approximately on time as indicated by the Flight Plan.

Meanwhile, here in the Control Center, one of the docking collar capture latches is here in the Control Center being examined by flight controllers trying to sort out why some of the latches apparently did not fully engage during the docking operation.
Spacecraft now 140,451 nautical miles [260,115 km] from Earth.
Velocity now is 3,683 feet per second [1,123 m/s].
Continuing to stand by on air-ground 2 for a conversation from the crew of Challenger.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, Gordo, we're on the top of 1-15, we're ready to go ahead and transfer the power and we'll give you a call.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, the LM Power circuit breaker is In.
What the - let me know what you want [garble] Okay, going to Off, Reset.
And, I got the old Master Alarm again.
The pressure's been - I don't know what it is.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Houston; we got a good transfer.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Got it again by hitting the panel.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, we've got considerable conversation going on here about that docking latch, and it's not at all settled yet.

But the primary thing we want to guard against is the possibility that it is malfunctioning and that we get it latched down on the ring and can't unlatch it, and, therefore, have problem with undocking, or possibly even prevent it.
So, we'd like you steer clear of that until we come up with a final solution.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And just one little other bit of information to let you know.
The handle itself is not free at this point to come on back down like it's - you know.
Like, if it were fully cocked, the handle itself would be free to come back down.
It is not free, I did not try to put a whole lot of pressure on it, but it's not free.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Glycol Pump 2 is On, it's been on about a minute, and we've got good talkbacks from batteries 1 and 4.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Jack, you can go ahead and switch on - onto high taps on 1 and 4.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, 1 and 4 are on high taps.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The LM has seven batteries, two in the ascent stage and the remainder in the decent stage.

Of these, two batteries (numbers 1 and 4) are used to power some systems in the LM from just before launch to the time when Ron connected up an umbilical from the CSM to the LM, thereby supplying power from the CSM's fuel cells.
Each battery consists of 20 cells connected in series to produce a voltage of around 28V.

However, when freshly charged, the battery voltage is somwhat higher than this so in order to supply the LM with approximately the correct voltage, a feed (or tap) is taken off batteries 1 and 4 from cell 17, presenting the circuit with the total voltage from only 17 cells.
This is known as the low voltage tap.
Once the crew gain entry into the LM, they switch batteries 1 and 4 to supply power from cell 20, producing voltage across all 20 cells.
This is known as the high voltage tap.
The 200-watt consumption of the LM will, by this time, have brought the overall voltage of these batteries down.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Well, you can cut off the decimal.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: No, wait a minute, I got to go get it.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Jack, you're cutting out on everything, and all I'm getting is the end of your transmission there.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, you - you were loud and clear after your first two words in every case, just like you were cutting out on VOX.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You got it all that time there now, do you read me too?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Let me adjust the squelch on this one here.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: I can't hear the squelch on any of them.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Jack, go VHF A Transmitter and Receiver, Off, and B Transmitter, Voice, and leave the Receiver, On.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Voice, On, and Off on B now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, loud and clear that time.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And my - my VHF Alpha had to go to 2 on the Squelch, and I'm in 3 on Bravo.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I took it B to 2.
So it's the same kind of thing I guess.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay for four minutes in voice, is low bit rate check [garble].
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Houston, how do you read Challenger?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You're loud and clear, although we have a lot of background noise.
Not sure whether that's getting through.
Do you know if Ron is still on VOX?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Ron has been speaking with the ground using a a more whereby his voice gets switched to the downlink merely by the action of speaking.
In other words, it is a voice-operated switch, or VOX.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: [Garble] Challenger to give you a call [garble].
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, stand by, and Challenger's going to give you a call on S-band, and VHF checks are both Go on A and B.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think our site lost lock on Challenger, we are getting some data.
We'll stand by for another check.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I heard him the first time.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, you ought to be getting in [garble] low bit rate.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You're loud and clear, with the background noise.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: They don't have a good uplink Gene.
And now I don't have any at all.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Houston, Challenger gave you a call, but he does not have any uplink signal strength at all.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, and we'd like him to hold this configuration right there, until we get the site straightened out and we understand what the problem is.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: He just got about 2.2 on the signal strength.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And tell them I heard their transmission to you.
[Pause.] I was at zero signal strength and I heard their transmission, at least one of them.
The first time they acknowledged, that I was going to do it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Did you shift my hose back?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: When you call me, it fluctuated down to about 1.6.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The background noise I've been mentioning, although I guess you haven't heard me mention it until now, is what we expect in Down Voice Backup and Low Bit Rate.
How have you read this transmission all the way through?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Gene, did he say "say again?" I got a little bit - oh he's got three point...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I've got 3.4 on the signal strength, and try me again.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You're coming in loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I know you tried to transmit.
I could just barely tell that.
I could see the signal strength vary down to 3.
You were modulating, apparently, but you're not getting through to me.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'm transmitting simul now in both S-band frequencies, and it sounds to us like for some reason when I transmit, the uplink signal starts to break up.
It's just the way you see it.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Still modulating, but I cannot read you.
Would you - would you tell America what you said?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yes, Gordy, we - we read you in America loud and clear on that simul.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: As soon as he starts trying to talk, the signal strength drops off about 0.4.
Now, I have had - I heard him loud and clear one time when he called you guys.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: America and Challenger, we're going to hand over to a different site and try that here in about 30 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: What else do we have to do, get some of these mags stowed?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We've got to stow the mags.
Here, put the PPK where it belongs.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Hello, Challenger; this is Houston through Bermuda.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Do you want to [garble] up the camera [garble] tow our LCG back there [garble].
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I think he answered us, but he was completely lost in the noise.
I could just barely make out some answers, somebody talking.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: If he did hear you, he didn't answer.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Can you tell Jack, ask him to verify he's on aft Omni?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I'll have him verify he's on aft Omni.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: [Garble] earlier when he was [garble] that he had good signal strength [garble] he would transmit [garble].
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: But on this last set of checks, we didn't hear anything at your end.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: There was one time earlier when he had zero signal strength where he said he could hear you transmitting to us very weak but clear.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You're loud - well, you're about 3 by, Gordy, but very clear.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're getting you about 1 by 1 in the midst of a lot of noise, but we could tell you're sounding much better.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Going up to you through Bermuda, and your - you should come down through Goldstone.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: [Pause.] Well, he started to say something; then he dropped off.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're going to go back to Goldstone, both up and down, and we'd like Jack to do step 406 on the checklist; in other words, go to Voice, S-Band Voice to Voice, the Biomed to Right.
And we're going to give it a try in the normal configuration.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Biomed, Right; and S-Band Voice to Voice.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Houston, You just came in loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And that time, you're loud and clear, Jack and sounds real good.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Let me say once again, way back when we first started and Gene was talking to you, I heard one of your answers, weak but clear, with zero signal strength showing here.
Now, that might have been through Ron's VOX, I don't know.
Since then, you've been able to modulate the signal strength, with occasional weak words.
And - and then, when that signal strength was up around 3.2, where it is now, then you came in loud and clear with a broadcast.
I answered you, you said about three words, then dropped off completely.
And then we - we changed configuration.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That correlates with what we've seen here in the way of signal strengths dropping in and out.
Stand by, and I'll see where we want to go from here.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We'd like you to do step 7.
We'll check out the telemetry High Bit Rate.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We're looking at the High Bit Rate for a minute here while we're waiting.
Would you check the Cross Tie circuit breakers, panel 16?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Cross Tie's Bus and Bal Loads, Open.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We'd like you to accomplish step number 8.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: How do you read the Challenger?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Pyrotechnic charges (also known as Explosive Devices, or EDs) are used to carry out various functions on the Lunar Module, particularly the separation of its descent and ascent stages.
Other functions include the operation of essential helium valves and the deployment of the landing legs.
Power to ignite the EDs comes from two redundant and independent batteries that have their open-circuit voltage periodically checked.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: ED Bats A and B are 37.2.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And go ahead with step 10, and then we'll go back and try to find out what's wrong with Down Voice Backup.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Go ahead and check the sequence camera, if you haven't already, Jack, and then we'll have some steps for you to check out what was wrong with Down Voice Backup.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Sequence camera - sequence camera is Go.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The sequence camera is also also known as a Data Acquisition Camera, a very NASA-esque name for a Maurer 16-mm movie camera.
This will be used to film the lunar descent and ascent.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And I'm ready for your step.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We'd like the Ranging switch to Off/Reset, and the Voice switch to Down Voice Backup, then give us a call.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Off/Reset on Range, and Down Voice Backup on Voice.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: On Down Voice Backup, how do you read?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Challenger, we read your transmission, but you're just about - just about lost in the noise.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, Houston; this is the Challenger.
You were loud and clear, loud and clear.
No different from the transmissions in Voice, and counting 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Would you go to High Bit Rate?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: How do you read High Bit Rate?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We'd like Biomed, Off now, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: How do you read the LM?
Down Voice Backup; Biomed, Off; PCM, Hi.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And that was hot mike, so that's working fine up here.
And you're loud and clear, also.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Talking to you Down Voice Backup; Biomed, Off; and PCM, Lo.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You're coming through 5 by at signal strength 3.2, and - all appearances up here, that previous problem was an uplink.
But I guess you were not reading me.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, I was - The answer is yes and no at various times.
We think, though, we have a good handle on the problem.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Are you ready for us to press on here, Gordy?
We're all stowed and - in pretty good shape in the LM.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We'd like you to check one more switch first before moving on.
Check the Updata Link switch to Off.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Want you to stand by a minute or two Jack, until we verify a funny-looking parameter here on that uplink switch.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: No, they want - they got something looking funny on the uplink - they're [garble] I guess on the uplink switch.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: They want to look at it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We were reading the data wrong.
You're clear to press on with the checklist now on page 1-20.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Geno, here is your jettison bag.
You can get back to Ron.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, S-band PR Off, VHF Off; I can hear you.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We just transferred to CSM power, and the caution warning light did go off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gordo, we're about wrapped up here in Challenger.
It looks like there's some life in - in the old bird, huh?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'll give you a call when we - when we get the hatch closed.
Ron's doing some housekeeping in the Command Module, and we're going to take it slow getting back in.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've got a couple of questions for you about those - about the latch.
Good old latch number 4 gave us a problem I guess, huh?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Ron, can you take a look at the latch number 4, and is the hook back away from the ring approximately one inch, as opposed to resting against the ring?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: America, Houston, did you copy my question?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We're going back to air-ground one.
transcript.speaker: MCC-H

tokens: Okay, I heard somebody going to air-ground one that time, and...
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Got a couple of questions on those - that latch number 4 if you can answer it for me.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We're back with you after some site changeover.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: On that number 4, latch number 4, is the hook backed away from the ring about 1 inch as opposed to resting against the ring?
Do you have about an inch clearance on that hook right now?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: In what position was the latch handle when the hook was first moved back, Ron?
Was it kind of like in a normal stroke-back, or was it just flopping back?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: No, when I first looked at it, the latch handle was - see, the hook itself was over the ring when I first looked at it.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: The hook was over the ring, and the - and the latch handle was flush, you know.
It looks just like a normal - hooked docking latch, you know.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: I can confirm that it did not fold back easily, because I - I checked it yesterday.
I didn't check - I checked to see that the hooks were over as well as where the handles would fold back, but I obviously didn't check to see whether that one was seated.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And the first thing you saw was the depression in the bungee.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: And the bungee is depressed about 3/8 - 3/8 to a ½ inch you know.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: You can push the - you can push the hook about an inch away from the ring, but the normal - just a resting position on the thing is about a finger's width or ½ inch from the ring.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: In other words, the loose - The hook is loose so it flops a little bit; you can move it back and forth.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: I can move it from about ½ inch to an inch from the edge of the ring - clearing the edge of the docking ring.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Ron, when you pulled the hook - when you pulled the handle back, did you reach and pull the hook back with it, or did it come back with the handle like a normal pullback then?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: No, the - the hook did not come back with the handle.
The hook did not come back with the handle.
Although, when I - when I pulled the handle back to - you know, which is - which is kind of a normally uncocked position - and then it wouldn't go any further, you know.
And then the latch itself - or the hook - the J-hook; I'll call it the hook.
I moved it off off the docking ring back to this position that was about an inch or ½ inch from the docking ring.
And it stays right there now.
And I can't push it back up into the docking ring at all.
Can't push the hook back up to the docking ring at all.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Our plan here is to leave it like it is.
And we're going to think about it tonight, and we'd like you to just keep pressing on.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: It'll clear the docking ring; no problem.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: While Ron's putting in the probe and getting the hatch back in, I'm going to go ahead and maneuver.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: 042:24:30 -This is Apollo Control at 42 hours, 27 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We're estimating the change of shift press conference in 15 minutes; 15 minutes at 6:15 Central Time in the small briefing room.
Spacecraft communicator now is Bob Overmeyer.
During this past checkout of the Lunar Module, we've had considerable difficulty in some of the communications configurations in the way the downlinks and uplinks were set up.
And some bit of difficulty in sorting out just what the nature of the problem in one of the docking latches is.
People will be examining this through the night and during the checkout of the LM tomorrow, or additional work in the LM, perhaps the thing can be sorted out.
Apollo 17 presently at 143,562 nautical miles [265,877 km] out from Earth, traveling at a velocity of 3,590 feet per second [1,094 m/s].
At 42:28 and standing by, this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Bob, are you all going to want Omni or High Gain on this?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're all locked up on the High Gain.
We'd just like you to leave it on High Gain.
The angles you see in the Flight Plan are in case it breaks lock.
That's what you need for - with this attitude.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We'll get the hatch in now.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: I'm not going to connect the umbilicals unless you really want to look at probe temperature.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: The hatch went in nominally, once the probe installation strut was stowed in the right position.
transcript.speaker: Evans
